questions.prompt: Which component arrangement creates an ideal inverting integrator?
options: A resistor at the input and a resistor in the feedback loop
A capacitor at the input and a resistor in the feedback loop
A resistor at the input and a capacitor in the feedback loop
A capacitor at the input and a capacitor in the feedback loop
questions.correctAnswer: A resistor at the input and a capacitor in the feedback loop
questions.explanation: The voltage across the feedback capacitor is the integral of the current forced into it by the input resistor.

questions.prompt: If a constant positive DC voltage is applied to the input of an ideal inverting integrator, what will the output look like?
questions.explanation: Integrating a constant value produces a linear line (ramp). Because it is an inverting integrator, the positive input produces a negative-going slope.

questions.prompt: What is the primary practical problem with an ideal integrator circuit?
options: It suffers from DC drift and will eventually saturate due to tiny offset voltages
It has an extremely low input impedance
questions.correctAnswer: It suffers from DC drift and will eventually saturate due to tiny offset voltages
questions.explanation: Because a capacitor blocks DC, there is no DC negative feedback. The op-amp continuously integrates its own tiny internal error voltages until it hits the supply rails.

questions.prompt: How is the integrator DC drift problem typically solved in a practical (lossy) integrator?
options: Adding a large resistor in parallel with the feedback capacitor
Adding a small capacitor in parallel with the input resistor
Replacing the op-amp with a BJT
questions.correctAnswer: Adding a large resistor in parallel with the feedback capacitor
questions.explanation: The parallel resistor provides a DC path for negative feedback, stabilizing the op-amp at extremely low frequencies (DC) while allowing it to act as an integrator at higher frequencies.

questions.prompt: Which component arrangement creates an ideal inverting differentiator?
options: A resistor at the input and a capacitor in the feedback loop
A capacitor at the input and a resistor in the feedback loop
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor at the input and a resistor in the feedback loop
questions.explanation: The current through the input capacitor is proportional to the rate of change of the input voltage. This current then flows through the feedback resistor to create the output voltage.

questions.prompt: If a perfect triangle wave is applied to the input of an ideal differentiator, what is the shape of the output?
questions.explanation: A triangle wave consists of segments with constant positive slope and constant negative slope. The derivative (rate of change) of a constant slope is a flat DC line. The alternating slopes create a square wave.

questions.prompt: What is the primary practical problem with an ideal differentiator circuit?
options: It draws zero current from the source
Its gain approaches infinity at high frequencies, heavily amplifying noise
questions.correctAnswer: Its gain approaches infinity at high frequencies, heavily amplifying noise
questions.explanation: Because the impedance of the input capacitor drops as frequency increases, high-frequency noise gets amplified immensely and swamps the useful low-frequency signal.

questions.prompt: What happens to the phase of a sine wave when it passes through an inverting differentiator?
options: It shifts by nearly 90 degrees (becoming a negative cosine wave)
questions.correctAnswer: It shifts by nearly 90 degrees (becoming a negative cosine wave)
questions.explanation: The mathematical derivative of sin(x) is cos(x) (a 90° shift forward). The inverting configuration adds a negative sign, resulting in -cos(x), meaning it's shifted by 90 degrees backwards.

questions.prompt: In the differentiator equation V_o = -RC(dV_in/dt), what happens if you double the value of the capacitor C?
options: The output voltage magnitude doubles for the same input signal
The time required to differentiate doubles
questions.correctAnswer: The output voltage magnitude doubles for the same input signal
questions.explanation: The multiplying factor is RC. Doubling C doubles the overall multiplication factor, producing twice as much voltage output for identical rates of change.

questions.prompt: Why can we state that the current through the input component equals the current through the feedback component (i_in = -i_feedback) in KCL analysis for both these circuits?
options: Because the op-amp's ideal input impedance is infinite, meaning no current flows into its internal terminals
Because the virtual ground node has zero resistance
Because of the conservation of energy law
questions.correctAnswer: Because the op-amp's ideal input impedance is infinite, meaning no current flows into its internal terminals
questions.explanation: The infinite input impedance rule (i_ = 0) is central to deriving almost every op-amp circuit equation. All current must bypass the op-amp and travel through the feedback path.
